assertions.text: Discount ladder, traveller eligibility, and ticket pricing are in separate tables. Rolling window counting as a fourth table is desirable.
assertions.evidence: Four distinct @TableTest methods: adultPensionerTierLadder (tier ladder), categoryEligibility (traveller eligibility), ticketPriceByZoneCategoryAndHistory (end-to-end pricing), rollingWindowCountsPurchasesWithinLast30Days (rolling window).

assertions.text: Tables focus on tier rules and eligibility, not on price arithmetic (zone_price × discount). The pricing table tests the end-to-end pipeline.
assertions.evidence: The tier ladder table only checks discountPercent from tierDiscountPercent(ticketCount); the category table only checks discountPercent; arithmetic appears only in the dedicated end-to-end pricing table.

assertions.text: Every tier (0%, 5%, 10%, …, 40%) has at least one row. Ideally with boundary values (4→5, 9→10, etc.) or value sets covering the full range per tier.
assertions.evidence: Tier ladder table has rows for 0%, 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25%, 30%, 35%, 40% with value sets covering both boundaries of each range: {1,4} for 0%, {5,9} for 5%, {10,14} for 10%, etc.

assertions.text: The 30-day rolling window boundary is tested: a ticket at exactly 30 days is included, at 31 days is excluded.
assertions.evidence: "Exactly on day 30 is included | [30] | 1" and "Day 31 is excluded | [31] | 0" rows in rollingWindowCountsPurchasesWithinLast30Days table.

assertions.text: Youth and students are explicitly shown as paying adult price with no discount. Not left ambiguous or omitted.
assertions.evidence: Category eligibility table: "Youth earns no discount regardless of count | YOUTH | {1, 40} | 0" and "Student earns no discount regardless of count | STUDENT | {1, 40} | 0". Also in pricing table: "Youth, full price, zone 2 | 2 | YOUTH | 30 | 0% | 75.00" and "Student, full price, zone 4 | 4 | STUDENT | 20 | 0% | 134.00".

assertions.text: Eligibility uses human-readable values (yes/no or equivalent) rather than raw true/false. TypeConverter or similar mechanism mentioned.
assertions.evidence: The response uses numeric discount percentages (0, 5, 10, etc.) and enum names (ADULT, CHILD, YOUTH, STUDENT) rather than yes/no human-readable eligibility values. No TypeConverter or similar mechanism is mentioned for converting human-readable strings.

assertions.text: Pricing table includes a discount column (e.g. 'Discount?') so the reader can trace: traveller + tickets → discount tier → price.
assertions.evidence: The pricing table includes "Discount tier?" column with values like "0%", "5%", "10%", etc. The @Description notes: "The Discount tier? column traces the intermediate step: which tier was applied before computing the price."

assertions.text: Ticket prices are arithmetically correct: zone_price × (1 − discount/100) for every pricing row.
Value sets in the tier table contain exactly the values that produce the same discount. No cross-tier contamination.

assertions.evidence: @DisplayName("Tier ladder — adults and pensioners"), @DisplayName("Category eligibility rules"), @DisplayName("End-to-end ticket price by zone, category, and purchase history"), @DisplayName("Rolling 30-day purchase window") present on all four test methods.

assertions.text: @Description is present on at least the class or the tier method, explaining the discount ladder structure.
assertions.evidence: @Description on the class explains the discount ladder structure. The tier method adultPensionerTierLadder also has @Description("Discount climbs in 5 percentage point steps per tier. Boundary values for both ends of each tier are tested.").

assertions.text: TypeConverterSources is used for at least one human-readable conversion (yes/no, or rolling window notation).
assertions.evidence: No @TypeConverterSources annotation appears anywhere in the response. The rolling window uses List<Integer> directly with numeric values, and eligibility uses enum names (ADULT, CHILD, etc.) without any TypeConverter mechanism.
